_comment: Hand-curated plain-language context + think-tank citations, keyed by metric id. Numbers are NOT stored here — fetch-spending.mjs pulls those from official APIs and merges these fields in.

federal-outlays.context: Total money the federal government actually spent during the fiscal year, across every agency and program. This is the headline 'spending' number.
federal-outlays.why: Outlays are the broadest measure of the size of government. Compare the trend against receipts (what the government took in) to see whether spending is growing faster than revenue.

federal-deficit.context: The gap between what the government spent and what it collected in a fiscal year. A positive number means it spent more than it took in and had to borrow the difference.
federal-deficit.why: Persistent deficits add to the national debt and to future interest costs. Watching the deficit trend is the single clearest signal of fiscal direction.

federal-receipts.context: Total revenue the federal government collected in the fiscal year, mostly individual income taxes, payroll taxes, and corporate taxes.
federal-receipts.why: Receipts are the other half of the deficit equation. If outlays rise while receipts stay flat, the gap is driven by spending, not a revenue shortfall.

national-debt.context: The total amount the federal government owes, accumulated from decades of deficits. Includes debt held by the public plus intragovernmental holdings.
national-debt.why: The debt is the running total of past borrowing. As it grows, so does the interest bill, which competes with every other spending priority.

interest-on-debt.context: What the government pays each year to service the national debt — gross interest across all Treasury securities. This has roughly doubled in a few years as rates and debt both rose.
interest-on-debt.why: Interest is now one of the largest single line items in the budget, rivaling defense. Every dollar of interest is a dollar not available for other priorities, and it grows automatically as debt compounds.

debt-held-by-public.context: The portion of the national debt owed to outside investors — individuals, funds, and foreign governments — as opposed to money the government owes itself (like the Social Security trust funds).
debt-held-by-public.why: Economists usually treat debt held by the public as the most meaningful debt measure, because it reflects actual borrowing from financial markets and drives interest costs.

spending-by-function.context: How federal spending splits across major purposes — Social Security, Medicare, defense, interest, and so on — for the most recent completed fiscal year, per USAspending.gov.
spending-by-function.why: The category mix shows where the money actually goes. Note USAspending uses a broader, account-level basis than the Treasury outlays figure, so its total is larger and the two won't reconcile exactly — read this for the relative shares, not the grand total.
